content.full_text:
SACRAMENTO OFFICE
STATE CAPITOL
P.O. BOX 942849
SACRAMENTO, CA 94249-0001
(916) 445-7448

DISTRICT OFFICE
1501 NORTH BUENA BOULEVARD
SUITE 201
FULLERTON, CA 92835
(714) 738-5853
(213) 697-0603

Assembly
California Legislature

ROSS JOHNSON
ASSEMBLYMAN, SIXTY-FOURTH DISTRICT

ASSEMBLY REPUBLICAN LEADER

March 27, 1990

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Scott:

There is much confusion regarding Proposition 73 and its impact on
elections. I believe this situation has been caused by the way the
FPPC has interpreted Proposition 73. The Commission has altered
its advice on numerous occasions, often taking diametrically
opposed positions on the same question.

The following questions represent a sampling of some of the issues
that must be resolved by the Commission. While I do not believe
any of these scenarios are permitted under the Political Reform
Act, it is crucial that the Commission give consistent advice so
that all candidates and officeholders operate under the same rules.

Under your interpretation may a candidate for a statewide office
send out a slate mailer paid by his/her campaign funds and include
the names of candidates running for other offices?

Under your interpretation may a candidate for local, legislative
or statewide office pay for a campaign brochure containing the
photograph of an elected representative or other candidate from
the district in which the brochure is received by the voters, if the
officeholder or candidate is identified only by name, not as a
candidate for office? Does your advice change if the officeholder
or candidate is identified as a candidate for a specific office?

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 27, 1990
Page Two

On a related matter, under your interpretation may a candidate or
officeholder use campaign funds to pay for a slate mailer urging
support or opposition of a state ballot measure? Is your advice
dependent upon whether the name of the candidate (who is paying for
the mailing) also appears on the slate mailer?     Would the
candidate be limited as to the size of the mailing? For example,
would a candidate for the 90th Assembly District be limited to only
sending the slate mailer to that particular Assembly district or
could that candidate finance a slate mailing to be sent statewide?
Conversely, can a state ballot measure committee pay for a slate
mailer and include the names of candidates? Can a state ballot
measure send out campaign brochures containing photographs of
elected representatives or candidates supporting the particular
measure if those brochures are earmarked for specific districts in
which the officeholder or candidate are running for election? Is
your advice dependent upon whether the officeholder or candidate
is identified only by name or as a candidate for office?
information on

My position is clear. The above mentioned scenarios violate both
the letter and spirit of Proposition 73. In regards to the
question as to whether one candidate can pay for a slate mailer
with campaign funds and include the name of another candidate, I
maintain this is a violation of the inter-candidate transfer ban
contained in Section 85304. In regards to slate mailers and ballot
measures, I also contend that Section 85202 prohibits candidates
from using campaign funds to pay for a slate mailer supporting or
opposing a state ballot measure. There is no question that
a payment would constitute a violation of the trust provisions of
Section 85202.

Candidates and officeholders already are looking for ways around
that the contribution limits of Proposition 73. Your advice will
determine whether they are successful or whether they have to learn
to live with the new rules. It is abundantly clear that
Proposition 73 is having a significant impact on the political arms
race in regards to legislative fundraising. Your advice on these
and similar questions will affect all elections this year.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

[signature: Ross Johnson]

ROSS JOHNSON
Assembly Republican Leader
